categories.name: JavaScript Core Concepts
categories.description: Fundamental concepts of the JavaScript programming language, including data types, functions, and execution context.

categories.name: Objects and Functions
categories.description: Working with objects, functions, prototypes, and classes in JavaScript.

categories.name: Asynchronous JavaScript
categories.description: Managing asynchronous operations with promises, async/await, and event loop.

categories.name: ES6+ Features
categories.description: Modern JavaScript features including destructuring, spread operators, and new data structures.

categories.name: JavaScript Methods
categories.description: Built-in methods for arrays, objects, strings, and other JavaScript data types.

categories.name: Error Handling and Debugging
categories.description: Understanding errors, debugging techniques, and error handling in JavaScript.

categories.name: Browser APIs
categories.description: Web APIs available in the browser environment including DOM, storage, and timers.

topics.name: Hoisting
topics.description: JavaScript's behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope during compilation.

topics.name: Scope and Closures
topics.description: Variable accessibility and function scope in JavaScript.

topics.name: Type Coercion
topics.description: Automatic or implicit conversion of values from one data type to another.

topics.name: Equality and Comparisons
topics.description: Understanding equality operators (== vs ===) and value comparison.

topics.name: Data Types and Structures
topics.description: Primitive and reference data types in JavaScript.

topics.name: 'this' Keyword
topics.description: Understanding the behavior of the 'this' keyword in different contexts.

topics.name: Prototypes and Inheritance
topics.description: JavaScript's prototypal inheritance model.

topics.name: Functions and Methods
topics.description: Different types of functions including regular, arrow, and generator functions.

topics.name: Classes and OOP
topics.description: ES6 class syntax and object-oriented programming in JavaScript.

topics.name: Object Manipulation
topics.description: Creating, modifying, and working with JavaScript objects.

topics.name: Event Loop and Concurrency
topics.description: Understanding JavaScript's event loop, call stack, and task queues.

topics.name: Promises
topics.description: Working with promises for asynchronous operations.

topics.name: Async/Await
topics.description: Modern syntax for handling asynchronous code.

topics.name: Timers and Intervals
topics.description: Working with setTimeout, setInterval, and scheduling.

topics.name: Destructuring
topics.description: Extracting data from arrays and objects using destructuring syntax.

topics.name: Spread and Rest Operators
topics.description: Using spread and rest operators for array and object manipulation.

topics.name: Symbols and Unique Identifiers
topics.description: Using Symbol primitive type for unique property keys.

topics.name: Map and Set
topics.description: ES6 collection data structures.

topics.name: Array Methods
topics.description: Built-in methods for array manipulation.

topics.name: String Methods
topics.description: Built-in methods for string manipulation.

topics.name: Object Methods
topics.description: Built-in methods for object manipulation.

topics.name: JSON Methods
topics.description: Working with JSON parsing and stringification.

topics.name: Error Handling
topics.description: Try/catch blocks and error management.

topics.name: Strict Mode
topics.description: JavaScript's strict mode and its implications.

topics.name: Storage APIs
topics.description: Browser storage mechanisms including sessionStorage and localStorage.

topics.name: DOM Events
topics.description: Event handling and propagation in the browser.

topics.name: Modules and Imports
topics.description: ES6 module system and import/export syntax.

questions.explanation: Variables declared with var are hoisted and initialized with undefined, while variables declared with let are hoisted but not initialized (temporal dead zone), causing a ReferenceError when accessed before declaration.
questions.hint: Consider the difference between var and let hoisting behavior.

questions.explanation: var is function-scoped, so the same i is shared across all setTimeout callbacks, logging 3 three times. let is block-scoped, creating a new i for each iteration, logging 0, 1, 2.
questions.hint: Consider the scope differences between var and let in loops.

questions.explanation: Regular functions have their own 'this' binding (referring to the object). Arrow functions inherit 'this' from the surrounding scope (global/window), where radius is undefined, resulting in NaN when used in calculations.
questions.hint: Examine how 'this' behaves differently in regular functions versus arrow functions.

questions.explanation: The unary plus converts true to 1. The logical NOT operator converts the truthy string 'Lydia' to false when checking if it's falsy.
questions.hint: Understand how unary operators convert types.

questions.explanation: mouse.bird.size attempts to access the 'size' property of undefined (mouse.bird is undefined). Bracket notation evaluates expressions first: mouse[bird.size] becomes mouse['small'], which returns true.
questions.hint: Distinguish between dot notation and bracket notation for object property access.

questions.explanation: Objects are assigned by reference. When d = c, both variables point to the same object in memory. Modifying the object through one reference affects the other.
questions.hint: Consider how objects are stored and referenced in JavaScript.

questions.explanation: new Number(3) creates an object wrapper, not a primitive number. == checks value equality after type coercion (true), while === checks both value and type (false). Both a === b and b === c are false because b is an object, not a number primitive.
questions.hint: Understand the difference between primitive values and object wrappers.

questions.explanation: Static methods are defined on the class itself, not on instances. They cannot be called on instances (freddie), only on the class (Chameleon.colorChange()).
questions.hint: Review the purpose and usage of static methods in classes.

questions.explanation: Due to the typo, 'greetign' becomes a property on the global object (window in browsers, global in Node.js). This creates an empty object on the global scope.
questions.hint: Consider what happens when you assign to an undeclared variable.

questions.explanation: Functions are objects in JavaScript. They can have properties added to them just like any other object.
questions.hint: Think about what type functions are in JavaScript.

choices.text: SyntaxError. You cannot add properties to a function this way.

questions.explanation: The getFullName method is added to the constructor function itself, not to its prototype. Instance methods must be added to the prototype to be accessible to instances.
questions.hint: Consider the difference between adding methods to a constructor vs. its prototype.

questions.explanation: When called with new, this refers to the new object. Without new, this refers to the global object, and the function doesn't return anything, so sarah is undefined.
questions.hint: Understand what happens when you forget the 'new' keyword with constructor functions.

questions.question_text: What are the three phases of event propagation?
questions.explanation: Event propagation occurs in three phases: Capturing (from window down to target), Target (reaching the target element), and Bubbling (from target back up to window).
questions.hint: Recall the order in which events travel through the DOM.

questions.question_text: All objects have prototypes. (True or False?)
questions.explanation: All objects have prototypes except for the base object (Object.prototype). The base object has no prototype, but all other objects inherit from it through the prototype chain.
questions.hint: Think about the top of the prototype chain.

questions.explanation: JavaScript performs type coercion when adding a number and a string. The number 1 is coerced to a string, resulting in string concatenation: '1' + '2' = '12'.
questions.hint: Consider how JavaScript handles the + operator with mixed types.

questions.explanation: Postfix increment (number++) returns the value before incrementing. Prefix increment (++number) increments first, then returns the new value. Starting at 0: first logs 0 (then becomes 1), prefix increments 1 to 2 and logs 2, final value is 2.
questions.hint: Understand the difference between prefix and postfix increment operators.

questions.explanation: Tagged template literals pass the string parts as an array as the first argument, followed by each expression value. Here, one receives the array of string parts, two receives 'Lydia', three receives 21.
questions.hint: Learn how tagged template literals work in JavaScript.

questions.explanation: Objects are compared by reference, not by value. Even though the objects have the same structure, they refer to different memory locations, so both equality checks return false.
questions.hint: Remember how JavaScript compares objects versus primitives.

choices.text: Hmm.. You don't have an age I guess

questions.explanation: The rest parameter collects arguments into an array. Arrays are objects in JavaScript, so typeof an array returns 'object'.
questions.hint: What is the data type of arrays in JavaScript?

questions.explanation: In strict mode, assigning to an undeclared variable throws a ReferenceError. Without strict mode, it would create a global variable.
questions.hint: Consider how strict mode affects variable assignment.

questions.question_text:
What is the value of sum?

```javascript
const sum = eval('10*10+5');
```
questions.explanation: eval() evaluates JavaScript code represented as a string. The expression '10*10+5' is evaluated to the number 105.
questions.hint: What does the eval() function do?

questions.question_text:
How long is data stored in sessionStorage accessible?

```javascript
sessionStorage.setItem('cool_secret', 123);
```
questions.explanation: sessionStorage data persists only for the duration of the page session. It is cleared when the tab or browser window is closed. localStorage persists until explicitly cleared.
questions.hint: Compare sessionStorage with localStorage.

choices.text: Forever, the data doesn't get lost.
When the user closes the tab.
When the user closes the entire browser, not only the tab.
When the user shuts off their computer.

questions.explanation: var allows redeclaration of the same variable within the same scope. The variable will hold the last assigned value. let and const do not allow redeclaration.
questions.hint: Understand the redeclaration rules for var, let, and const.

questions.explanation: Object keys are always strings (or Symbols). obj.hasOwnProperty('1') and obj.hasOwnProperty(1) both check for the string key '1'. Set elements are not converted to strings; 1 and '1' are different values.
questions.hint: Consider how object keys and Set elements are treated differently.

questions.explanation: When an object has duplicate keys, the last value assigned to that key overwrites previous ones. The key retains its original position in the object.
questions.hint: What happens when you define the same property multiple times in an object literal?

questions.question_text: The JavaScript global execution context creates two things for you: the global object, and the "this" keyword. (True or False?)
questions.explanation: When JavaScript code starts running, it creates the global execution context which sets up the global object (window in browsers, global in Node.js) and the 'this' keyword (which refers to the global object in the global context).
questions.hint: Think about what is available globally when your script starts.

questions.explanation: The continue statement skips the current iteration when i equals 3. The loop logs 1, 2, then skips 3, and continues with 4.
questions.hint: How does the 'continue' statement affect loop execution?

questions.explanation: String.prototype allows adding methods to all strings. Primitive strings are temporarily wrapped in String objects when methods are called, giving them access to prototype methods.
questions.hint: Consider how primitive values can access methods.

questions.explanation: Object keys are automatically converted to strings. When using an object as a key, it becomes '[object Object]'. Both assignments set a['[object Object]'] to different values, with the last one (456) persisting.
questions.hint: What happens when you use an object as a property key?

questions.explanation: setTimeout's callback is asynchronous and goes to the Web API, then to the task queue. It only executes after the call stack is empty, after synchronous code (foo and baz) completes.
questions.hint: Consider how the event loop handles setTimeout callbacks.

questions.question_text:
What is the event.target when clicking the button? ```html
```
questions.explanation: event.target refers to the deepest nested element that triggered the event - the element that was actually clicked. In this case, it's the button element.
questions.hint: What does event.target represent in event handling?

choices.text: An array of all nested elements.

questions.question_text:
When you click the paragraph, what's the logged output? ```html

Click here!

```
questions.explanation: Event handlers execute in the bubbling phase by default. The event bubbles from the target element (p) up through its ancestors, so 'p' is logged first, then 'div'.
questions.hint: What is the default phase for event handlers?

questions.explanation: call() immediately invokes the function with the specified this context. bind() returns a new function with the this context bound, but does not invoke it.
questions.hint: What is the difference between call() and bind()?

questions.explanation: sayHi() returns the result of an immediately invoked function expression (IIFE) that returns 0. typeof 0 returns 'number'.
questions.hint: Break down the function execution step by step.

questions.explanation: The falsy values are: undefined, null, NaN, false, '' (empty string), 0, -0, and 0n. Object wrappers (new Number, new Boolean) are always truthy, even when wrapping falsy values.
questions.hint: Remember the list of falsy values in JavaScript.

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
console.log(typeof typeof 1);
```
questions.explanation: typeof 1 returns 'number'. typeof 'number' returns 'string' because the operand is a string.
questions.hint: Evaluate the expression from the inside out.

questions.explanation: When you assign to an index beyond the array's length, JavaScript creates empty slots (not undefined values) between the last element and the new index. These empty slots appear differently depending on the environment.
questions.hint: What happens when you set an array element at an index larger than the current length?

questions.explanation: The catch parameter x is block-scoped to the catch block and shadows the outer x. Inside catch, x is set to 1 (overriding the error object), and y is set to 2. Outside catch, outer x remains undefined, while y is 2 (from the catch block's scope).
questions.hint: Consider variable scoping within try-catch blocks and parameter shadowing.

questions.question_text: Everything in JavaScript is either a...
questions.explanation: JavaScript has two main categories of types: primitives (boolean, null, undefined, number, bigint, string, symbol) and objects (including functions, arrays, dates, etc.). Primitives are immutable and have no methods, though they can be temporarily wrapped for method access.
questions.hint: Think about the fundamental type system in JavaScript.

questions.explanation: reduce starts with initial value [1,2]. First iteration: acc=[1,2], cur=[0,1] → concat → [1,2,0,1]. Second iteration: acc=[1,2,0,1], cur=[2,3] → concat → [1,2,0,1,2,3].
questions.hint: Trace through the reduce method step by step with the given initial value.

questions.explanation: !! converts a value to its boolean equivalent. null and '' are falsy, so !! returns false. 1 is truthy, so !! returns true.
questions.hint: What does the double NOT operator do?

questions.question_text:
What does the setInterval method return in the browser?

```javascript
setInterval(() => console.log('Hi'), 1000);
```
questions.explanation: setInterval returns a unique numeric ID that can be used with clearInterval() to stop the interval from continuing to execute.
questions.hint: How do you cancel a setInterval?

questions.question_text:
What does this return?

```javascript
[...'Lydia'];
```
questions.explanation: Strings are iterable in JavaScript. The spread operator spreads each character of the string into individual elements in an array.
questions.hint: What happens when you spread a string?

questions.explanation: Generator functions pause execution at each yield. First next() returns the first yielded value (10). Second next() continues from where it paused and yields the next value (10 * 2 = 20).
questions.hint: How do generator functions and the yield keyword work?

questions.explanation: Promise.race settles with the first promise that settles (resolves or rejects). secondPromise resolves after 100ms, faster than firstPromise's 500ms, so 'two' is logged.
questions.hint: How does Promise.race() determine which promise to return?

questions.explanation: members[0] holds a copy of the reference to the original object. Setting person to null only changes the person variable, not the array element which still references the original object.
questions.hint: What happens to array elements when you change the original variable?

questions.explanation: The for...in loop iterates over enumerable property names (keys) of an object, not their values.
questions.hint: What does for...in iterate over in an object?

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
console.log(3 + 4 + '5');
```
questions.explanation: Addition is left-associative. First, 3 + 4 evaluates to 7 (number). Then, 7 + '5' results in type coercion: the number 7 is converted to a string and concatenated with '5', giving '75'.
questions.hint: Consider operator associativity and type coercion.

questions.question_text:
What is the value of num?

```javascript
const num = parseInt('7*6', 10);
```
questions.explanation: parseInt parses until it encounters a character that isn't a valid digit in the specified radix. The '*' is not a valid digit in base 10, so it stops parsing at that point, returning only 7.
questions.hint: How does parseInt handle non-numeric characters?

questions.explanation: The if condition is true for all numbers, so the function returns early with no explicit return value. Functions without a return statement return undefined. map creates a new array with these undefined values.
questions.hint: What does a function return when there's no return statement?

questions.explanation: Objects are passed by reference, so modifying member affects the original person object. Strings are passed by value, so modifying year only affects the local copy, not the original birthYear variable.
questions.hint: How are objects and primitives passed to functions?

questions.explanation: throw can throw any expression, not just Error objects. In this case, it throws the string 'Hello world!', which is caught and logged as e in the catch block.
questions.hint: What types of values can be thrown in JavaScript?

choices.text: SyntaxError: can only throw Error objects
Oh no an error: Hello world!

questions.explanation: When a constructor function explicitly returns an object, that object is returned instead of the default this object. The returned object { make: 'Maserati' } overrides the constructor's default behavior.
questions.hint: What happens when a constructor function returns an object?

questions.explanation: y = 10 creates a global variable y (no declaration). let x = y declares block-scoped x. x is not accessible outside the IIFE, so typeof x returns 'undefined'. y is global and exists, typeof y returns 'number'.
questions.hint: Understand the difference between assignment and declaration.

questions.explanation: The first bark() call works normally. delete removes the bark method from the prototype. The second call tries to invoke undefined as a function, throwing a TypeError.
questions.hint: What happens when you delete a method from a prototype?

choices.text: "Woof I am Mara", "Woof I am Mara"

questions.explanation: Set objects store unique values. Duplicate values are automatically removed when creating a Set from an iterable.
questions.hint: How does Set handle duplicate values?

questions.explanation: ES6 module imports are read-only bindings. You cannot modify the value of an imported module directly. Attempting to do so throws an error.
questions.hint: Can you modify imported values in ES6 modules?

questions.explanation: delete cannot delete variables declared with var, let, or const. name is declared with const, so delete returns false. age is an implicit global (no declaration), so it can be deleted, returning true.
questions.hint: What can and cannot be deleted with the delete operator?

questions.explanation: Array destructuring assigns the first element of the array to the variable y. This is equivalent to const y = numbers[0].
questions.hint: How does array destructuring work?

questions.explanation: The spread operator copies enumerable properties from the user object into the new admin object, combining them with its existing properties.
questions.hint: What does the spread operator do with objects?

questions.explanation: Properties added with defineProperty are non-enumerable by default (unless enumerable: true is specified). Object.keys only returns enumerable properties, so 'age' is not included.
questions.hint: What is the default enumerability of defineProperty?

questions.explanation: JSON.stringify's second parameter (replacer) can be an array of property names to include in the output. Only 'level' and 'health' are included, 'username' is omitted.
questions.hint: What does the replacer array do in JSON.stringify?

questions.explanation: Postfix increment returns the original value before incrementing. num1 gets 10 (num was 10, then incremented to 11). When num1 (10) is passed to increasePassedNumber, number++ returns 10, then increments the local copy to 11 (doesn't affect num1).
questions.hint: Remember how postfix increment works and how arguments are passed.

questions.explanation: Default parameters are evaluated at call time. First two calls create new objects from value, so they log 20 each (10*2). Third and fourth calls pass the actual value object, which persists modifications: first modifies 10→20, then 20→40.
questions.hint: When are default parameter values evaluated? How does object mutation work?

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
[1, 2, 3, 4].reduce((x, y) => console.log(x, y));
```
questions.explanation: Without an initial value, reduce uses the first element as the accumulator (x=1) and starts with the second element as current value (y=2). The callback doesn't return a value, so accumulator becomes undefined for subsequent iterations.
questions.hint: What happens when reduce's callback doesn't return a value?

choices.text: 1 undefined and 2 undefined and 3 undefined and 4 undefined

questions.explanation: In derived classes, you must call super() before accessing this. Constructor 2 correctly calls super(name) first, then sets this.size. The others either don't call super or call it incorrectly.
questions.hint: What are the rules for constructor in derived classes?

questions.explanation: ES6 imports are hoisted and executed before the importing module's code. All imported modules are parsed and executed first, in the order of the dependency graph.
questions.hint: What is the execution order with ES6 imports?

questions.explanation: Number and Boolean return primitive values when called as functions (not constructors). Symbols are always unique - each Symbol() call creates a completely new symbol, even with the same description.
questions.hint: Are Symbols equal even with the same description?

questions.explanation: padStart adds padding to reach the specified total length. 'Lydia Hallie' has length 12, so padStart(13) adds 1 space at the beginning. If target length is less than or equal to current length, no padding is added.
questions.hint: How does padStart work when target length is less than string length?

choices.text: " Lydia Hallie", " Lydia Hallie" ("[13x whitespace]Lydia Hallie", "[2x whitespace]Lydia Hallie")
" Lydia Hallie", "Lydia Hallie" ("[1x whitespace]Lydia Hallie", "Lydia Hallie")

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
console.log('🥑' + '💻');
```
questions.explanation: The + operator concatenates strings. Emojis are Unicode characters represented as strings, so they are concatenated normally.
questions.hint: How does JavaScript handle emojis in strings?

choices.text: A string containing their code points

questions.explanation: First call to next() without arguments yields the first yield expression value. Second call passes 'Yes' to replace the yield expression, which becomes the answer variable, allowing the function to continue to the next return.
questions.hint: How do you pass values back into a generator function?

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
console.log(String.raw`Hello\nworld`);
```
questions.explanation: String.raw is a tag function that returns the raw string form, ignoring escape sequences like \n. Backslashes are interpreted as literal backslashes, not as escape characters.
questions.hint: What does String.raw do to escape sequences?

questions.explanation: Async functions always return a Promise. The function hasn't resolved yet when data is assigned, so data is a pending Promise. To get the resolved value, you need to use .then() or await.
questions.hint: What does an async function return?

questions.explanation: Array.push() returns the new length of the array, not the array itself. After pushing 'apple' to ['banana'], the array has length 2, so 2 is returned.
questions.hint: What does the push method return?

questions.explanation: Object.freeze makes an object immutable - properties cannot be added, removed, or changed. shape references the same frozen object, so attempting to modify x fails silently (or throws in strict mode).
questions.hint: What does Object.freeze do to an object?

questions.explanation: This code uses destructuring with renaming: firstName is extracted and assigned to a new variable myName. The original property name firstName is not available as a variable - only myName exists.
questions.hint: What variable names are created when destructuring with renaming?

questions.explanation: A pure function always returns the same output for the same input and has no side effects. sum(a,b) always returns a+b with no external dependencies or modifications, making it a pure function.
questions.hint: What are the characteristics of a pure function?

questions.explanation: This is a memoized function. The cache persists across calls due to closure. First call with 10 calculates and caches. Second call with 10 returns from cache. 5*2 evaluates to 10, which is already in cache.
questions.hint: How does closure help with memoization?

questions.explanation: for...in iterates over enumerable property names (array indices). for...of iterates over iterable values (array elements). Arrays are objects with indices as keys and values as elements.
questions.hint: What's the difference between for...in and for...of?

questions.explanation: Array elements can contain expressions, which are evaluated when the array is created. The expressions are computed to their results: 3, 2, and 0.5.
questions.hint: When are expressions in array literals evaluated?

questions.explanation: When a function parameter is not provided, it defaults to undefined. The template literal converts undefined to the string 'undefined', resulting in 'Hi there, undefined'.
questions.hint: What is the default value of missing function parameters?

questions.explanation: First log: data.getStatus() uses the object's status property ('🥑'). Second log: .call(this) changes the context to the setTimeout's this, which in arrow functions inherits from outer scope (global), where status='😎'.
questions.hint: What does 'this' refer to in different contexts and with call()?

questions.explanation: city is assigned the value of person.city (undefined), then reassigned to 'Amsterdam'. This doesn't affect the person object because city is a separate variable, not a reference to the object property.
questions.hint: Does assigning a variable affect the original object?

questions.explanation: const and let are block-scoped. message is declared inside if/else blocks and is not accessible outside those blocks. Trying to return it throws a ReferenceError.
questions.hint: Where are const and let variables accessible?

questions.question_text:
What kind of information would get logged?

```javascript
fetch('https://www.website.com/api/user/1')
  .then(res => res.json())
  .then(res => console.log(res));
```
questions.explanation: Each .then receives the return value of the previous .then. The first .then returns res.json(), which is a Promise that resolves to the parsed JSON. The second .then receives that parsed JSON value.
questions.hint: What value is passed between .then callbacks?

choices.text: The result of the fetch method.
The result of the second invocation of the fetch method.
The result of the callback in the previous .then().

questions.explanation: !!name converts the name value to its boolean equivalent. This returns true for any truthy value passed to the function, which matches the requirement of setting hasName to true without explicitly passing true.
questions.hint: How can you convert any value to its boolean equivalent?

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
console.log('I want pizza'[0]);
```
questions.explanation: Strings can be accessed using bracket notation with indices, like arrays. Index 0 returns the first character of the string, which is 'I'.
questions.hint: Can you access characters in a string by index?

questions.explanation: Default parameters can reference previous parameters. Here, num2 defaults to num1. With one argument (10), num1=10, num2 defaults to num1 (10), so 10+10=20.
questions.hint: Can default parameters reference other parameters?

questions.explanation: import * creates an object containing all exports. The default export becomes a property named 'default', and named exports become properties with their names.
questions.hint: How does import * handle default and named exports?

questions.explanation: Classes in JavaScript are syntactic sugar over constructor functions. Instances created with new are objects, so typeof returns 'object'.
questions.hint: What is the type of a class instance?

questions.explanation: push() returns the new length of the array, not the array itself. newList becomes the number 4 (the length after pushing 4). Trying to call push on a number throws a TypeError.
questions.hint: What does push() return?

questions.explanation: Regular functions have a prototype property (an object with a constructor). Arrow functions do not have a prototype property - they cannot be used as constructors.
questions.hint: Do arrow functions have a prototype property?

questions.explanation: Object.entries returns an array of [key, value] pairs. The for...of loop with destructuring assigns each pair's key to x and value to y.
questions.hint: What does Object.entries return?

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
function getItems(fruitList, ...args, favoriteFruit) {
  return [...fruitList, ...args, favoriteFruit]
}

getItems(["banana", "apple"], "pear", "orange")
```
questions.explanation: The rest parameter must be the last parameter in a function definition. Having parameters after ...args is invalid syntax and throws a SyntaxError.
questions.hint: Where can a rest parameter appear in a function definition?

questions.explanation: Due to Automatic Semicolon Insertion (ASI), a semicolon is inserted after 'return', making it a standalone statement. The function returns undefined, and a + b is never executed.
questions.hint: How does Automatic Semicolon Insertion affect return statements?

choices.text: a is bigger, 6 and b is bigger, 3
a is bigger, undefined and b is bigger, undefined

questions.explanation: Classes can be reassigned like variables. Person is reassigned to AnotherPerson before instantiation, so the instance uses the new class definition with name 'Sarah'.
questions.hint: Can you reassign a class to another class?

questions.explanation: Symbol keys are not enumerable, so they don't appear in Object.keys(). However, when logging the entire object, the Symbol property is visible.
questions.hint: Are Symbol properties enumerable?

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
const getList = ([x, ...y]) => [x, y]
const getUser = user => { name: user.name, age: user.age }

const list = [1, 2, 3, 4]
const user = { name: "Lydia", age: 21 }

console.log(getList(list))
console.log(getUser(user))
```
questions.explanation: getList destructures the array: x gets first element, y gets rest as array. getUser's arrow function with {} is interpreted as a block, not an object return. To return an object, it needs parentheses: ({ name: user.name, age: user.age }).
questions.hint: How do you return an object from an arrow function?

questions.explanation: name is a string, not a function. Attempting to invoke it as a function throws a TypeError because strings are not callable.
questions.hint: What happens when you try to call a non-function?

questions.question_text:
What is the value of output?

```javascript
const output = `${[] && 'Im'}possible!
You should${'' && `n't`} see a therapist after so much JavaScript lol`;
```
questions.explanation: [] is truthy, so [] && 'Im' evaluates to 'Im'. '' is falsy, so '' && `n't` evaluates to '' (falsy value). The template literal concatenates: 'Im' + 'possible!' + 'You should' + '' + ' see a therapist...'
questions.hint: How does the && operator work with truthy and falsy values?

choices.text: possible! You should see a therapist after so much JavaScript lol
Impossible! You should see a therapist after so much JavaScript lol
possible! You shouldn't see a therapist after so much JavaScript lol
Impossible! You shouldn't see a therapist after so much JavaScript lol

questions.explanation: The || operator returns the first truthy operand, or the last operand if all are falsy. {} is truthy, so one = {}. All operands for two are falsy, so two = '' (last). [] is truthy, so three = [].
questions.hint: How does the logical OR (||) operator determine its return value?

questions.explanation: In firstFunction, synchronous code runs first ('second'), then microtask (promise resolution). In secondFunction, await pauses execution until promise resolves, so 'I have resolved!' logs before 'second'.
questions.hint: How does await differ from .then() in execution order?

choices.text: I have resolved!, second and I have resolved!, second
second, I have resolved! and second, I have resolved!
I have resolved!, second and second, I have resolved!
second, I have resolved! and I have resolved!, second

questions.explanation: The + operator performs different coercions: 1+2=3 (number), 'Lydia'+2='Lydia2' (string concatenation), {}+2 converts object to string '[object Object]' then concatenates: '[object Object]2'.
questions.hint: How does the + operator handle different types?

questions.question_text:
What is its value?

```javascript
Promise.resolve(5);
```
questions.explanation: Promise.resolve returns a Promise that is resolved with the given value. It returns a Promise object in the fulfilled state with value 5.
questions.hint: What does Promise.resolve return?

questions.explanation: Both person1 (argument) and person2 (default parameter) reference the same object in memory. Strict inequality (!==) checks reference equality, so they are considered equal.
questions.hint: When comparing objects, what does strict equality check?

questions.explanation: colorConfig.colors attempts to access a property named 'colors' on colorConfig, which doesn't exist (returns undefined). Trying to access index 1 of undefined throws a TypeError.
questions.hint: What happens when you try to access a property that doesn't exist on an object?

questions.question_text:
What is its value?

```javascript
console.log('❤️' === '❤️');
```
questions.explanation: Emojis are represented by Unicode code points. The same emoji always has the same Unicode representation, so comparing two identical emoji strings returns true.
questions.hint: Are emojis represented consistently in JavaScript strings?

questions.explanation: Only splice modifies the original array by deleting/replacing elements. map, filter, slice return new arrays. find returns an element. reduce returns a reduced value.
questions.hint: Which array methods mutate the original array?

questions.explanation: food[0] is a primitive string value. When assigned to info.favoriteFood, it copies that value, not a reference to the array element. Changing info.favoriteFood doesn't affect the original array.
questions.hint: Does assigning an array element to a variable create a reference?

questions.question_text:
What does this method do?

```javascript
JSON.parse();
```
questions.explanation: JSON.parse converts a JSON string into a JavaScript value or object. It's the inverse of JSON.stringify.
questions.hint: What is the purpose of JSON.parse?

choices.text: Parses JSON to a JavaScript value
Parses a JavaScript object to JSON
Parses any JavaScript value to JSON
Parses JSON to a JavaScript object only

questions.explanation: The getName function has its own name variable declared with let. Due to the temporal dead zone, accessing name before its declaration (even though there's an outer name) throws a ReferenceError.
questions.hint: What happens when you try to access a let variable before declaration?

questions.explanation: yield returns the entire array as one value. yield* delegates to another iterable, yielding each item individually. So one yields the array, two yields 'a' first.
questions.hint: What's the difference between yield and yield*?

questions.question_text:
What is the output of the following code?

```javascript
console.log(`${(x => x)('I love')} to program`);
```
questions.explanation: The expression inside ${} is evaluated first. The arrow function (x => x) is immediately invoked with 'I love', returning 'I love'. This is then interpolated into the template literal.
questions.hint: How are expressions evaluated in template literals?

questions.explanation: setInterval creates a timer that continues running even if the object referencing it is set to null. The arrow function maintains a closure over the timer, preventing garbage collection. The interval must be cleared with clearInterval.
questions.hint: Does setting an object to null stop its setInterval?

choices.text: The setInterval callback won't be invoked
The setInterval callback gets invoked once
The setInterval callback will still be called every second
We never invoked config.alert(), config is null

questions.explanation: Map keys are compared by reference. The key is the specific function myFunc. 'greeting' is a different value (string). () => 'greeting' creates a new function with a different reference, so only myMap.get(myFunc) returns the value.
questions.hint: How does Map compare keys for objects and functions?

questions.explanation: changeAge(person) mutates the original person object (age becomes 22). changeAgeAndName() is called with no argument, so it uses a new object copy from person, not affecting the original.
questions.hint: When does a default parameter create a new object vs. using the passed argument?

questions.explanation: The spread operator ... spreads array elements as individual arguments. sumValues(...[1,2,3]) passes 1,2,3 as separate arguments, summing to 6.
questions.hint: How do you pass array elements as individual arguments?

questions.explanation: num += 1 increments num to 2, then the expression evaluates to 2. list[2] returns the element at index 2, which is '🥰'.
questions.hint: What is the value of the expression (num += 1)?

questions.explanation: Optional chaining (?.) short-circuits and returns undefined if the left side is nullish. person.pet exists, so pet.name returns 'Mara'. pet.family doesn't exist, so returns undefined. getFullName exists and is called. member doesn't exist, so ReferenceError.
questions.hint: How does optional chaining handle non-existent properties vs. non-existent variables?

questions.explanation: indexOf returns 0 when 'banana' is found at index 0. 0 is falsy, so the else block executes, logging 'We don't have to buy bananas!'
questions.hint: What does indexOf return when an item is at the beginning of an array?

choices.text: We don't have to buy bananas!

questions.explanation: The language property is defined as a setter only, with no getter. Accessing it directly returns undefined, even though it can be used for assignment.
questions.hint: What happens when you access a property that only has a setter?

questions.explanation: typeof name returns 'string'. !'string' (with ! operator) converts 'string' to boolean false. false === 'object' is false, false === 'string' is false. Both comparisons return false.
questions.hint: What is the precedence of the ! operator compared to ===?

questions.explanation: This is a curried function. Each arrow function returns another function, capturing the outer x and y through closure. The innermost function has access to all three values.
questions.hint: How does closure work with nested arrow functions?

questions.explanation: This is an async generator. It yields promises. for await...of waits for each promise to resolve before logging the resolved value (1,2,3).
questions.hint: How does for await...of work with async generators?

questions.explanation: The function expects a single object argument with properties x, y, z. Passing three separate numbers doesn't match the expected parameter, so x, y, z are undefined.
questions.hint: What happens when you pass arguments that don't match the destructuring pattern?

questions.explanation: Intl.NumberFormat formats numbers according to locale. 130 as 'mile-per-hour' becomes '130 mph'. 300 as USD currency becomes '$300.00'.
questions.hint: What does Intl.NumberFormat do with different style options?

choices.text: The driver drove 130 and has to pay 300
The driver drove 130 mph and has to pay $300.00
The driver drove undefined and has to pay undefined
The driver drove 130.00 and has to pay 300.00

questions.explanation: This uses destructuring assignment to assign the value '💀' to spookyItems[3]. The parentheses are needed because {} at start of statement is treated as a block. This adds a new element at index 3.
questions.hint: How does destructuring assignment work with array elements?

questions.explanation: Number.isNaN checks if the value is exactly NaN (type 'number' and value NaN). isNaN coerces the value to a number first, then checks if it's NaN. name coerces to NaN, so isNaN returns true.
questions.hint: What's the difference between Number.isNaN and global isNaN?

questions.explanation: Inside getInfo, there's a const declaration for randomValue. Due to the temporal dead zone, accessing randomValue before its declaration (even though there's an outer variable) throws a ReferenceError.
questions.hint: What happens when you have a variable with the same name in nested scopes?

questions.explanation: myPromise resolves successfully, so the try block logs the resolved value. No error occurs, so catch is skipped. The finally block always executes, logging 'Oh finally!'.
questions.hint: When does the finally block execute?

choices.text: Woah some cool data Oh finally!

questions.explanation: flat(1) flattens one level deep. It concatenates the first-level nested array ['✨', '✨', ['🍕', '🍕']] into the main array, but leaves the second-level array intact.
questions.hint: How does the depth parameter in flat() work?

questions.explanation: counterTwo is a reference to the same object as counterOne. All increments affect the same object. 2 increments on counterOne + 1 increment via counterTwo = 3 total.
questions.hint: Do counterOne and counterTwo reference the same object?
